content.text: Operational Runbook Completeness Criteria

A runbook completeness score is a quantitative assessment of whether an operational runbook is fit for production use. It replaces subjective review ('looks good to me') with a scored rubric that an engineer can evaluate in under 15 minutes. The score drives a Go/No-Go gate: a runbook below the production threshold cannot be included in a launch checklist until gaps are resolved.

The goal of a completeness score is not to produce paperwork. It is to surface the specific gaps that will cause a runbook to fail during an incident — before the incident, not during it. Every point lost on the rubric represents a predictable failure mode.

This sub-subtopic covers the five-category scoring rubric, the scoring algorithm implementation, the gate thresholds and their rationale, the automated lint tool that checks runbooks in CI, and the resolution process for runbooks that fail the gate.

attrs.alt: Runbook completeness score model: five categories (Structure, Commands, Context, Freshness, Accessibility) scored out of 100

content.text: The Five-Category Scoring Rubric
Each category scores a different dimension of runbook quality. The weights reflect the relative impact of each gap on incident resolution time.
Category 1: Structure (25 points)
Structure points are awarded when the required sections are present and non-empty. An empty section header scores zero for that section.
Section
Points
Scoring rule
Prerequisites
Must list required tools, access roles, and environment setup
Trigger criteria
Must describe which alert or condition prompts this runbook
Impact statement
Must describe customer-facing effect during the incident
Numbered steps
Steps must be numbered and each step must be a discrete action (not a paragraph)
Verification criteria
Must specify metric or output that confirms recovery
Escalation path
Must name a person or team to page if steps fail
Category 2: Commands (25 points)
Command points are awarded when every command in the runbook is executable as written, with documented expected output.
Criterion
Points
Scoring rule
No placeholder values (YOUR_VALUE, <fill-in>)
All placeholders replaced with either real values or env var references
Expected output documented for each command
Each command step has a comment or note showing what a successful run looks like
Commands runnable in one terminal session
No undocumented context switches; no commands that require opening a second window without explanation
Tool versions pinned or documented
kubectl, helm, aws-cli versions are documented; commands use flags compatible with documented version
Category 3: Context (20 points)
Context points are awarded when the runbook gives the operator enough background to make judgment calls — distinguishing expected from unexpected behavior, and knowing when to escalate.
Criterion
Points
Scoring rule
Trigger clearly differentiates this runbook from others
A reader seeing the alert can identify this runbook without ambiguity
Impact is quantified
Not 'some customers are affected' — 'all requests with X characteristic fail'
Rollback decision logic present
States when to rollback vs. fix-forward vs. wait
Known false positives documented
Lists conditions that look like triggers but are not (e.g., cold-start spike)
Category 4: Freshness (15 points)
Freshness points degrade over time. A runbook that has not been touched since a major infrastructure change is operationally stale even if it was complete when written.
Criterion
Points
Scoring rule
Updated within 90 days
Full 5 pts if last-modified within 90 days; 3 pts if 90–180 days; 0 pts if > 180 days
Drill executed within 90 days
Full 5 pts if drill record exists within 90 days; 0 otherwise
No stale command patterns
Commands pass syntax check; no deprecated flags; no services that no longer exist
Category 5: Accessibility (15 points)
Accessibility points ensure the runbook can be found and used by an engineer who was not involved in authoring it.
Criterion
Points
Scoring rule
Linked from the alert's annotation
Alert PrometheusRule or PagerDuty policy contains a direct runbook URL
All required access is documented and grantable
Prerequisites include IAM roles / k8s contexts that can be granted via self-service or a named approver
No single-person knowledge dependency
Runbook is understandable by a qualified engineer who was not involved in the feature; no 'ask alice' steps
Score Thresholds
Score Range
Status
Action
Production-ready
Runbook approved for launch checklist; review at next quarterly drill
Approved with gaps
Runbook approved; gaps filed as P2 tickets with 30-day resolution SLA
Conditional approval
Runbook approved only if gap owner is online during Day-1 launch window
Not approved
Runbook must be updated before it can be included in a launch checklist; launch blocked

The 80-point threshold is calibrated to the cost of a bad runbook in production. A runbook scoring 79 has too many undocumented gaps to trust an on-call engineer to fill them at 3am. The conditional 70–79 tier exists for cases where the gap owner can compensate in real time — but it is not a free pass.
Automated Runbook Linter

The completeness score can be computed automatically for Markdown runbooks. The linter parses the document structure, checks for required sections, validates command syntax, and checks freshness from Git history.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# runbook_lint.py — score a runbook and report gaps
import re, subprocess, sys
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def score_runbook(path: str) -> dict:
    content = Path(path).read_text()
    scores = {}
    gaps = []

    # ── Category 1: Structure ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    required_sections = {
        "Prerequisites": 4,
        "Trigger": 5,
        "Impact": 3,
        "Steps": 6,
        "Verification": 5,
        "Escalation": 2,
    }
    struct_score = 0
    for section, pts in required_sections.items():
        pattern = rf"##\s+.*{section}.*"
        match = re.search(pattern, content, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            # Check section is non-empty (next section starts or EOF)
            section_start = match.end()
            next_heading = re.search(r"\n##\s", content[section_start:])
            section_body = content[section_start:section_start + (next_heading.start() if next_heading else 500)]
            if len(section_body.strip()) > 20:
                struct_score += pts
            else:
                gaps.append(f"Section '{section}' is present but empty (+{pts} pts available)")
        else:
            gaps.append(f"Section '{section}' is missing (+{pts} pts available)")
    scores["structure"] = struct_score

    # ── Category 2: Commands ────────────────────────────────────────────────
    cmd_score = 0
    placeholders = re.findall(r'<[A-Z_]+>|YOUR_[A-Z_]+|\$\{[A-Z_]+\}', content)
    if not placeholders:
        cmd_score += 6
    else:
        gaps.append(f"Unfilled placeholders: {placeholders[:3]} (+6 pts if resolved)")

    # Check for expected output comments
    code_blocks = re.findall(r'```bash(.*?)```', content, re.DOTALL)
    blocks_with_output = sum(1 for b in code_blocks if "# Expected" in b or "# Output:" in b)
    if code_blocks and blocks_with_output / len(code_blocks) >= 0.75:
        cmd_score += 7
    else:
        gaps.append(f"Only {blocks_with_output}/{len(code_blocks)} command blocks have expected output (+7 pts if ≥75% do)")
    scores["commands"] = cmd_score

    # ── Category 4: Freshness ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    fresh_score = 0
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%ci", "--", path],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.stdout.strip():
        last_modified = datetime.fromisoformat(result.stdout.strip()[:19])
        days_since = (datetime.utcnow() - last_modified).days
        if days_since <= 90:
            fresh_score += 5
        elif days_since <= 180:
            fresh_score += 3
            gaps.append(f"Runbook last updated {days_since} days ago (< 90 for full 5 pts)")
        else:
            gaps.append(f"Runbook last updated {days_since} days ago — likely stale (+5 pts if updated)")
    scores["freshness"] = fresh_score

    # ── Category 5: Accessibility ───────────────────────────────────────────
    access_score = 0
    if "http" in content and "runbook" in content.lower():
        access_score += 5  # assume linked from alert
    if re.search(r"(iam|role|kubectl|context|access|permission)", content, re.IGNORECASE):
        access_score += 5
    if "ask alice" not in content.lower() and "contact me" not in content.lower():
        access_score += 5
    scores["accessibility"] = access_score

    total = sum(scores.values())
    return {"path": path, "total": total, "breakdown": scores, "gaps": gaps}

def main():
    path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else None
    if not path:
        print("Usage: runbook_lint.py <runbook.md>")
        sys.exit(1)

    result = score_runbook(path)
    print(f"\nRunbook: {result['path']}")
    print(f"Score: {result['total']} / 100")
    print(f"  Structure:    {result['breakdown'].get('structure', 0)} / 25")
    print(f"  Commands:     {result['breakdown'].get('commands', 0)} / 25")
    print(f"  Freshness:    {result['breakdown'].get('freshness', 0)} / 15")
    print(f"  Accessibility:{result['breakdown'].get('accessibility', 0)} / 15")

    if result['gaps']:
        print("\nGaps found:")
        for g in result['gaps']:
            print(f"  - {g}")

    threshold = 80
    if result['total'] >= threshold:
        print(f"\nRESULT: PASS ({result['total']} >= {threshold})")
        sys.exit(0)
    else:
        print(f"\nRESULT: FAIL ({result['total']} < {threshold}) — resolve gaps before launch")
        sys.exit(1)

main()
CI Integration

# .github/workflows/runbook-lint.yml
name: Runbook Completeness Gate
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'runbooks/**/*.md'

jobs:
  lint-runbooks:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0   # needed for git log freshness check

      - name: Lint changed runbooks
        run: |
          CHANGED=$(git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD | grep 'runbooks/.*\.md')
          FAILED=0
          for f in $CHANGED; do
            echo "--- Scoring: $f ---"
            python3 scripts/runbook_lint.py "$f" || FAILED=1
          done
          exit $FAILED

      - name: Post score summary as PR comment
        if: always()
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            // Parse scores from previous step output and post as PR comment
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: process.env.RUNBOOK_SCORES || "See CI output for runbook scores."
            })
Common Completeness Gaps and How to Fix Them
Gap
Category
Typical cause
Fix
Steps contain 'check if...' without specifying what to do for each outcome
Structure
Author wrote notes, not a runbook
Split into branching numbered steps: Step 3a / Step 3b with explicit action for each path
Commands have <CLUSTER_NAME> unfilled
Commands
Copy-pasted from a template without customization
Replace with actual cluster name or with $CLUSTER_NAME env var + document where to set it
No expected output on kubectl commands
Commands
Author knows the output by heart; didn't think to write it
Run the command in staging, copy the output, add as comment
Trigger section says 'when something goes wrong'
Context
Author was vague to avoid over-specifying
Name the specific alert: 'When alert RolloutLatencyP1 fires in Alertmanager'
Runbook not updated after Kubernetes namespace rename
Freshness
Infrastructure change with no runbook update requirement
Add runbook review to infrastructure PR template
No link from PagerDuty alert to runbook
Accessibility
Runbook added after alert was created
Edit PagerDuty service; add runbook URL to the alert body or custom details
Section exists but has only a single sentence
Structure
Section added to satisfy checklist without real content
Fill with the minimum viable content that makes the section actionable
Runbook Review Process

A completeness score is a necessary condition for runbook approval, but not sufficient. A scored runbook must also pass a peer review where a qualified engineer who did not author it reads every step and confirms they could execute it without external help.
Author runs automated linter and achieves score ≥ 80
Author opens a PR; CI runs linter and posts score as PR comment
A reviewer who is not the author and was not involved in the feature reads the runbook from top to bottom
Reviewer executes at least one non-destructive command from the runbook to confirm access and expected output
Reviewer approves or requests changes with specific line comments on gaps
Author resolves gaps, re-runs linter, and re-requests review
Approved runbook is merged; runbook URL is added to the relevant alert annotation
Reviewer Checklist
Can I identify which alert triggers this runbook without asking the author?
Can I execute Step 1 right now with my current access? If not, is the access gap documented in Prerequisites?
Is the expected output of each command written down?
Would I know when I'm done? (Verification section passes this test if yes)
If verification fails, do I know who to page?
Are there any steps that require knowledge not in this document?
Summary

A runbook completeness score converts subjective assessment into a quantitative gate that can be enforced in CI. The five categories — Structure, Commands, Context, Freshness, and Accessibility — each capture a distinct failure mode that causes runbooks to fail during incidents. Scores below 80 are not production-ready; scores of 80–89 are approved with tracked gaps; scores of 90+ are production-ready with no blockers. The automated linter makes this gate cheap to apply to every runbook PR. The peer review process ensures that a passing score reflects a runbook that a fresh engineer can actually execute — not just one that is structurally complete on paper.
25 pts: Structure — six required sections, each non-empty and actionable
25 pts: Commands — no placeholders, expected output documented, no undocumented context switches
20 pts: Context — trigger named, impact quantified, rollback decision logic present, false positives documented
15 pts: Freshness — updated within 90 days, drill executed within 90 days, no stale commands
15 pts: Accessibility — linked from alert, access documented, no single-person knowledge dependency
CI gate: runbook_lint.py scores every changed runbook in PRs; score < 80 blocks merge
